title: No saved data for this water
subtitle: You're offline and we don't have a cached guide for here yet. These general tips still help.

sections.heading: What this means
bullets: Live reports and the full AI guide need internet—or visit this spot online once so we can save intel for next time.

sections.heading: Quick rig ideas
bullets: Match depth to flow: faster water often needs more weight or a deeper nymph rig.
Morning and evening often favor dry-dropper or visible dries; bright midday can push fish subsurface.

sections.heading: If dries aren't working
bullets: Add a small dropper (midge, BWO nymph, or perdigon) under your dry.
Switch to a full nymph rig with enough weight to tick bottom in the seam.
Try a smaller or slimmer profile; sometimes color matters less than silhouette.

sections.heading: If nymphing is slow
bullets: Adjust depth first—most refusals are depth and speed, not fly name.
Try less weight and a longer drift, or more weight and a shorter, tighter line.
Rest a run after a few drifts; let fish reset.

sections.heading: Tippet and size
bullets: Clear, low water: finer tippet and smaller flies often help.
Stained or higher flows: you can go slightly larger and heavier.

sections.heading: Reading water
bullets: Seams between fast and slow current are prime holding lies.
Foam lines and structure (rocks, wood) concentrate food.
Tailouts and buckets below riffles are classic feeding lanes.

sections.heading: Safety & regs
bullets: Check regulations, closures, and private water before fishing.
Wade within your limits—high or cold flows increase risk.
